Hormone Control of Hunger, Satiation & Satiety

Block 5 · Dr. Tamara King · UNE COM M1
📍 PLOs + LOs Mapped 🧪 Case Study: Jack 💊 Pharmacology: GLP-1 RA 🔪 Bariatric Surgery
Core Concepts Gut Hormones Adipose Hormones Arcuate Nucleus GI → Arcuate Leptin → Arcuate Leptin Resistance Gastric Bypass Case Study
01

Core Concepts: Hunger, Satiation & Satiety PLO

🔑 Definitions

Hunger — the signal to start eating (orexigenic drive)

Satiation — the signal to stop eating during a meal

Satiety — the feeling of satisfaction between meals that delays return of hunger

🍽️ Orexigenic (Stimulate Food Intake)

  • Ghrelin — the only known orexigenic gut hormone
  • Activates NPY/AgRP neurons
  • Released when stomach is empty

🚫 Anorexigenic (Inhibit Food Intake)

  • PYY — L-cells of gut
  • GLP-1 — L-cells of gut & CNS
  • CCK — duodenal I-cells
  • Leptin — adipose tissue
02

Gut Hormones — Synthesis, Secretion & Effect PLO

Each hormone must be understood by: site of synthesis, secretion trigger, secretion pattern, and effect on food intake.

Orexigenic

Ghrelin

Source:Stomach (fundus), minor from intestine
Receptor:GHSR (Gq GPCR)
Pattern:↑ 1–2 hr before meal; peaks just before eating; ↓ within 1 hr after meal
Trigger:Empty stomach; suppressed by nutrients (carbs & protein > fat)
Action:↑ NPY/AgRP neurons → hunger
Anorexigenic

PYY (Peptide YY)

Source:L-cells of ileum, colon (highest in large bowel & rectum)
Receptor:Y2R (Gi GPCR) — inhibits NPY/AgRP
Pattern:Phase I: rapid (~15 min, neural); Phase II: ~2 hr post-meal (nutrient delivery to colon)
Trigger:Fat & protein content (most potent triggers)
Action:↓ NPY/AgRP; acts at arcuate nucleus → satiation
Anorexigenic

GLP-1 (Glucagon-like Peptide 1)

Source:L-cells of distal gut; α-cells of pancreatic islets; CNS neurons
Origin:Cleaved from proglucagon (same as glucagon)
Receptor:GLP-1R (Gs GPCR)
Pattern:Phase I: neural (before nutrients reach distal gut); Phase II: free fatty acids in intestinal lumen
Release:Proportional to calories ingested
Action:↑ POMC/CART neurons; acts on vagus nerve → satiation
Anorexigenic

CCK (Cholecystokinin)

Source:Duodenal I-cells and jejunal mucosa
Origin:Cleaved from CCK propeptide
Receptor:CCK1R (Gs GPCR) on vagus nerve
Trigger:Luminal lipids, proteins, amino acids
Action:Activates vagus → NTS (nucleus of solitary tract) → satiation signal
Note:Does NOT act directly on arcuate nucleus — vagus only
⚙️ Ghrelin Secretion Pattern (High-Yield)

Ghrelin is unique — it is the only gut hormone that increases appetite. It rises pre-prandially, peaks just before eating, and drops after the meal. Carbohydrates and proteins suppress ghrelin more effectively than fats — clinically relevant for dietary counseling.

GI Signal Routes to the Brain

Route 1: Direct to Arcuate Nucleus (Hypothalamus)

PYY / GLP-1 → Bloodstream → Arcuate Nucleus → Satiation

Route 2: Via Vagus Nerve

CCK / GLP-1 → CCK1R / GLP-1R on Vagus → NTS (medulla) → Satiation
03

Adipose Hormones — Leptin & Adiponectin PLO

Leptin

  • Source: White adipose tissue (WAT)
  • Signal type: Adipokine (cytokine-like)
  • Receptor: Cytokine receptor → Jak-STAT signaling
  • Effect: Anorexigenic — inhibits appetite, promotes satiety
  • Levels: Proportional to total fat mass (↑ fat → ↑ leptin)
  • Key action: Inhibits NPY/AgRP; stimulates POMC/CART neurons
  • CNS sites: Hypothalamus, NTS, ventral tegmental area (reward)
  • Low leptin = starvation signal; triggers food-seeking behavior

Adiponectin

  • Source: White adipose tissue
  • Receptor: GPCR family
  • Effects: Anti-inflammatory, insulin-sensitizing
  • Protects against diabetes and atherosclerosis
  • Enhances fatty acid catabolism
  • Regulates blood glucose
  • HMW form: correlates with glucose uptake and ↓ central obesity
  • Key point: Leptin and adiponectin generally have opposing effects
⚠️ High-Yield Distinction

Leptin vs. Ghrelin: Leptin is released from adipose (long-term energy stores) while ghrelin is released from the stomach (short-term meal-timing). Leptin signals energy sufficiency; ghrelin signals immediate hunger.

04

Arcuate Nucleus of the Hypothalamus PLOLO

🔑 The Regulatory Center

The arcuate nucleus is the master integrator of hunger and satiety. It receives input from the GI tract (ghrelin, PYY, GLP-1), adipose (leptin), and pancreas (insulin), and outputs to second-order neurons that drive eating behavior.

POMC/CART Neurons — Anorexigenic Satiation

  • POMC = pro-opiomelanocortin (precursor)
  • Cleaved to produce α-MSH (melanocyte-stimulating hormone)
  • α-MSH acts on MC4R (Gs GPCR) → ↑ cAMP → suppresses appetite
  • CART = cocaine & amphetamine-regulated transcript; peptides released from same neurons
  • Activation = decreased food intake, increased energy expenditure
  • Stimulated by: GLP-1, leptin, PYY (indirectly via AgRP inhibition)

AgRP/NPY Neurons — Orexigenic Hunger

  • NPY = neuropeptide Y — most potent stimulator of food intake
  • NPY acts via Y1R (Gi GPCR) → ↓ cAMP → increases hunger
  • AgRP = agouti-related peptide — inverse agonist at MC4R
  • AgRP blocks the satiety effect of α-MSH at MC4R
  • Also inhibits POMC neurons directly
  • Activation = increased food intake
  • Stimulated by: Ghrelin (via GHSR, Gq GPCR)
⚙️ MC4R — The Key Integration Point LO

MC4R (melanocortin-4 receptor) is a Gs GPCR on second-order hypothalamic neurons. It is the pivot point for appetite control:

  • α-MSH (from POMC neurons) → agonist at MC4R → Gs → ↑ cAMP → appetite suppressed
  • AgRP (from AgRP/NPY neurons) → inverse agonist at MC4R → opposes α-MSH → appetite increased
  • An inverse agonist binds the same receptor but produces the opposite pharmacological effect compared to the agonist
05

GI Hormone Interactions with Arcuate Nucleus LO

Ghrelin → Arcuate (Hunger Pathway)

Empty stomach → Hunger

Stomach empty → ghrelin released from fundus
Ghrelin binds GHSR (Gq GPCR) on AgRP/NPY neurons in arcuate nucleus
↑ NPY release → Y1R (Gi) on second-order neurons → ↓ cAMP → hunger
↑ AgRP release → binds MC4R as inverse agonist → blocks α-MSH satiety signal
Ghrelin also inhibits POMC/CART neurons directly

Net effect: Hunger + food-seeking behavior

PYY & GLP-1 → Arcuate (Satiation Pathway)

Food intake → Satiation

PYY (active form: PYY₃₋₃₆) released from L-cells; acts on Y2R (Gi GPCR) on AgRP/NPY neurons → inhibits AgRP/NPY neurons
GLP-1 released from L-cells; acts on GLP-1R (Gs GPCR) on POMC neurons → stimulates POMC/CART neurons → ↑ α-MSH
↑ α-MSH at MC4R → Gs → ↑ cAMP → appetite suppressed
GLP-1 also acts on vagus nerve → NTS → additional satiation signal

Net effect: Satiation (stop eating) + reduced hunger between meals

💡 Mnemonic: "PG suppress the hungry NPY"

PYY and GLP-1 work together to silence NPY/AgRP neurons (PYY via Y2R-Gi) and boost POMC neurons (GLP-1 via Gs), both pointing toward satiation. CCK takes a different route entirely — straight to the vagus nerve.

06

Leptin Effects on Arcuate Nucleus LO

🔑 How Leptin Signals Satiety

Leptin reflects long-term energy stores (adipose mass), not meal-to-meal fluctuations. It acts via the leptin receptor (LEPR), a cytokine receptor that signals through the Jak-STAT pathway.

Leptin's Dual Action on the Arcuate Nucleus

↑ POMC/CART Neurons (Activate)

Leptin binds LEPR on POMC/CART neurons → Jak-STAT signaling → ↑ POMC transcription → ↑ α-MSH → MC4R → appetite suppressed

↓ AgRP/NPY Neurons (Inhibit)

Leptin also directly inhibits AgRP/NPY neurons → ↓ NPY and AgRP release → less hunger drive → appetite suppressed

📌 Leptin Deficiency (Rare: <1 in 1 million)

Congenital leptin deficiency causes early-onset severe obesity with physiological signs of starvation: hyperphagia (extreme hunger), decreased immune function, and hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (because leptin normally signals to the brain that adequate energy is available for reproduction). Leptin replacement corrects all these abnormalities.

07

Leptin Resistance & Obesity LO

⚠️ The Obesity Paradox

In obesity, leptin levels are elevated (more fat → more leptin produced), yet the brain fails to respond appropriately. This is leptin resistance — the most common mechanism underlying obesity-related appetite dysregulation.

Feature
Leptin Deficiency
Leptin Resistance
Frequency
Very rare (<1:1M)
Very common (obese patients)
Leptin levels
↓↓↓ (absent/very low)
↑↑ (high, but ineffective)
Mechanism
Cannot produce leptin
Receptors/signaling impaired
Effect
Brain thinks starvation
Brain ignores satiety signal
Treatment
Leptin replacement (works!)
Lifestyle; GLP-1 RA; surgery

Factors Contributing to Leptin Resistance & Weight Gain

  • Hyperleptinemia itself — chronically elevated leptin downregulates leptin receptors (receptor desensitization)
  • Inflammation — obesity-associated chronic low-grade inflammation (adipokine dysregulation) impairs LEPR/Jak-STAT signaling
  • Impaired leptin transport across the blood-brain barrier (BBB) — reduced access to the hypothalamus
  • Increased endoplasmic reticulum (ER) stress in hypothalamic neurons
  • SOCS3 upregulation — suppressor of cytokine signaling 3 inhibits Jak-STAT pathway downstream of LEPR
  • Dysfunctional adipose tissue — visceral fat ≠ subcutaneous fat; visceral adipokine profile is pro-inflammatory
  • High-fat/high-calorie diet — directly impairs hypothalamic leptin signaling
  • Sleep deprivation — alters leptin/ghrelin balance (↓ leptin, ↑ ghrelin)
🏥 Vicious Cycle of Leptin Resistance

Weight gain → ↑ fat → ↑ leptin secretion → receptor desensitization → ↓ leptin effect → ↑ appetite → ↑ weight gain. The system that should prevent obesity is broken by obesity.

08

Bariatric Surgery & Gut Hormone Changes LO

🔑 Key Insight

Weight loss from bariatric surgery is not explained by restriction or malabsorption alone. The major mechanism is altered gut hormone signaling: enhanced nutrient delivery to the distal gut triggers exaggerated release of GLP-1 and PYY.

Types of Bariatric Surgery

Sleeve Gastrectomy

~80% of stomach removed, leaving a sleeve-shaped stomach (~150–200 mL capacity). Reduces ghrelin-producing fundus. Not a bypass — pylorus preserved, no dumping syndrome.

Effect on ghrelin: ↓↓ (fundus removed)

Roux-en-Y Gastric Bypass (RYGB)

Most commonly performed. Small pouch connected directly to ileum, bypassing stomach/duodenum/proximal jejunum. Biliopancreatic secretions mix with nutrients in the ileum.

Effect: ↓ absorption length; dumping syndrome possible (pylorus bypassed)

Hormonal Changes After RYGB

HormoneChange after RYGBReasonEffect on Appetite
Ghrelin ↓ or no change* Fundus excluded from food stream; less stretch/nutrient exposure ↓ Hunger drive
PYY ↑↑↑ Rapid nutrient delivery to distal ileum/colon → exaggerated L-cell stimulation ↑ Satiation (↓ NPY/AgRP)
GLP-1 ↑↑↑ Same — enhanced hindgut nutrient delivery → L-cell hyper-response ↑ Satiation (↑ POMC, vagus)
CCK Variable / ↑ Altered fat/protein delivery to duodenum ↑ Satiation via vagus
Leptin ↓ (gradually) Fat mass decreases post-op Leptin resistance may improve as fat ↓
💡 Net Effect after RYGB

Patients experience increased satiation after meals (not just less hunger). The dramatic rise in PYY and GLP-1 suppresses NPY/AgRP activity and boosts POMC/α-MSH signaling, producing a sustained reduction in appetite well beyond what caloric restriction alone would achieve.

ASMBS 2022 Indications

  • BMI ≥35 kg/m² regardless of co-morbidities — recommended
  • BMI 30–34.9 kg/m² with metabolic disease — should be considered
  • Type 2 diabetes with BMI 30–35 — strongly recommended
  • Asian population: thresholds lowered (≥25 clinical obesity; ≥27.5 consider surgery)
09

Case Study — Jack CASE

Patient Profile

Jack, 49 M. BMI 34.2 kg/m² (obese). Waist circumference 114 cm. Fasting glucose 110 mg/dL (prediabetes range). HbA1c 7.6% (above normal). BP 150/90 mmHg (high). Cholesterol borderline high; TG high; HDL low. Family Hx of T2DM. Trace proteinuria. Lifestyle changes recommended; no medication yet.

Chief complaint: Cannot lose weight despite increased activity. Wife reports he is hungrier than before and "gulps down his food." (Eating quickly → inadequate time for satiation signals to develop.)

Q1. What is the most likely cause of Jack's obesity? ▼
Answer: E

E. Decreased function of Leptin (Leptin resistance)

Jack is obese (BMI 34.2), so his adipose tissue is producing large quantities of leptin — this makes leptin deficiency (D) very unlikely. In obesity, leptin levels are typically elevated, but the hypothalamus fails to respond, i.e., leptin resistance.

Why not the other options?

  • A (↑ ghrelin): While ghrelin may contribute, it is not the primary driver — ghrelin abnormalities are a component of the picture, not the root cause in a typical obese patient.
  • B/C (↓ GLP-1/PYY or ↓ CCK): These may be relatively insufficient in obesity, but decreased satiation signaling is a secondary consequence, not the primary driver. CCK deficiency specifically is not the leading mechanism.
  • D (leptin deficiency): This is a genetic condition occurring in <1:1 million people — not the typical obese patient presentation.
💡 Clinical Reasoning

Obese patients have paradoxically high leptin but impaired leptin signaling. Jack "gulps down his food" — consistent with poor satiation (satiation hormones like GLP-1 and CCK need time to signal; eating quickly bypasses these cues) — but the chronic driving force is leptin resistance.

Q2. What has happened to Jack's Leptin levels? ▼
Answer: A — Increased

Leptin is secreted by white adipose tissue in proportion to fat mass. Jack has a BMI of 34.2 with excess adiposity (waist 114 cm). More fat → more leptin secretion.

The key point: his leptin levels are high, but his hypothalamus is resistant to the signal. It's analogous to insulin resistance in type 2 diabetes — high hormone levels, impaired response.

Q3. If GLP-1 receptor agonists are prescribed — 4 proposed mechanisms of weight loss, sites of action, and effects on NPY/CART and POMC signaling ▼
Answer — 4 Mechanisms

Sites of Action

  • Arcuate nucleus (hypothalamus) — GLP-1R on POMC/CART neurons and AgRP/NPY neurons
  • Vagus nerve — GLP-1R on vagal afferents → NTS → satiation
  • Stomach — delays gastric emptying
  • Pancreas — stimulates insulin secretion, inhibits glucagon

4 Proposed Weight Loss Mechanisms

Central appetite suppression: GLP-1R agonists activate POMC/CART neurons → ↑ α-MSH → MC4R-Gs → appetite suppressed. Simultaneously inhibit AgRP/NPY neurons.
Vagal satiation signaling: Activate GLP-1R on vagal afferents → signal to NTS in brainstem → early satiation signal during meals.
Delayed gastric emptying: Slows stomach emptying → prolonged distension → delayed return of hunger; nutrients released slowly to small bowel → prolonged PYY/CCK secretion.
Improved insulin sensitivity / glucose control: Glucose-dependent insulin release → better glycemic control → reduced lipogenesis and hunger signaling downstream.

Effect on NPY/CART and POMC Signaling

POMC/CART Neurons

↑ Activity — GLP-1R on POMC neurons (Gs) → ↑ POMC transcription → ↑ α-MSH → ↑ MC4R activity → appetite suppressed

NPY/AgRP Neurons

↓ Activity — GLP-1 indirectly suppresses NPY/AgRP neurons → ↓ NPY → ↓ Y1R activation; ↓ AgRP → less MC4R inverse agonism → less hunger

Q4. What factors associated with Leptin Resistance impact weight gain? ▼
Answer

See Section 07 — Leptin Resistance above for full details. Key factors:

  • Receptor desensitization from chronically elevated leptin levels
  • Obesity-associated inflammation (inflammatory cytokines impair Jak-STAT signaling in hypothalamic neurons)
  • Impaired BBB transport of leptin → less reaches the arcuate nucleus
  • SOCS3 upregulation — negative regulator of LEPR/Jak-STAT
  • ER stress in hypothalamic neurons → impaired leptin signal transduction
  • High-fat diet — directly impairs hypothalamic leptin sensitivity
  • Hyperinsulinemia (Jack's prediabetes) — insulin and leptin signaling share pathways; insulin resistance may promote leptin resistance
  • Visceral adiposity (waist 114 cm) — visceral fat has different adipokine secretion pattern than subcutaneous fat; more inflammatory
🏥 Jack's Specific Risk Factors

Jack has central obesity (waist 114 cm), prediabetes (fasting glucose 110 mg/dL, HbA1c 7.6%), hypertriglyceridemia, low HDL, and hypertension — this is essentially metabolic syndrome. Each of these is both a consequence and a driver of leptin resistance.

Q5. Jack can't tolerate GLP-1 (nausea/diarrhea). He wants Gastric Bypass Surgery. Which surgery? What happens to hormones? Will he be hungry? ▼
Answer

Which Surgery?

Given Jack's BMI of 34.2 with metabolic complications (prediabetes, hypertension, dyslipidemia, proteinuria), he would most likely be a candidate for Roux-en-Y Gastric Bypass (RYGB) — the most commonly performed bariatric surgery. The 2022 ASMBS guidelines recommend bariatric surgery for BMI 30–34.9 with metabolic disease.

Note: sleeve gastrectomy is an alternative, but RYGB specifically fits the "gastric bypass" request and is the gold standard for type 2 diabetes/prediabetes patients due to its metabolic effects.

Hormone Changes

HormoneDirectionMechanism
Ghrelin↓Fundus excluded from food stream → ↓ hunger drive
PYY↑↑↑Exaggerated hindgut delivery → L-cell hyperstimulation
GLP-1↑↑↑Same — rapid nutrient delivery to distal gut
CCK↑Altered fat delivery to duodenum
Leptin↓ (over time)Fat mass decreases → less adipose → less leptin secreted; leptin resistance may improve

Net Effect on Appetite

💡 Predicted Outcome: Increased Satiation

Jack would experience increased satiation after meals. The dramatic rise in PYY (↓ AgRP/NPY) and GLP-1 (↑ POMC/CART) resets the hypothalamic hunger/satiety balance. He would feel full sooner, eat less, and experience less inter-meal hunger. He would NOT remain hungry — he would have increased satiation.

Arcuate nucleus summary post-RYGB: ↑ GLP-1 → ↑ POMC activity → ↑ α-MSH → ↑ MC4R activity. ↑ PYY → ↓ AgRP/NPY via Y2R. ↓ Ghrelin → less activation of NPY/AgRP. Net: appetite suppressed.

10

Master Summary Table

Hormone Source Trigger Type Arcuate Target Route
Ghrelin Stomach fundus Empty stomach Orexigenic ↑ AgRP/NPY via GHSR (Gq) Blood + vagus
PYY L-cells (ileum, colon) Fat & protein Anorexigenic ↓ AgRP/NPY via Y2R (Gi) Blood → arcuate
GLP-1 L-cells + CNS + pancreatic α-cells Calories, free fatty acids Anorexigenic ↑ POMC/CART via GLP-1R (Gs) Blood + vagus
CCK Duodenal I-cells, jejunum Lipids, proteins, AA Anorexigenic Vagus → NTS only (not direct arcuate) Vagus only
Leptin White adipose tissue ↑ Fat mass Anorexigenic ↑ POMC; ↓ AgRP/NPY via LEPR (Jak-STAT) Blood → arcuate
